text:
The Artemis 2 mission is generating exactly the response you'd expect from the 'truth-seeking community'. It's all fake. It's all CGI. Nobody went to the moon in 1969 and nobody's going now. Case closed.

And maybe they're right. I have my best guesses, but I genuinely don't know for sure. But what I do know is that the speed and certainty with which people dismiss it tells me more about the psychology of the person than it does about the event itself. And that is what I am interested in.

When someone watches a rocket launch and their immediate response is "fake" with the same unshakable conviction that the mainstream has when they say "trust the science"... what exactly is the difference? You've replaced one set of unquestionable conclusions with another.

I thought we were here to ask questions. I thought the whole point was to think critically, hold things loosely, and resist the pull of certainty in either direction. When did "I can't know for sure" become taboo when it comes to certain topics?
And what if some of these conspiracy narratives are themselves part of a deeper psychological operation? If you wanted to undermine a civilisation's belief in its own achievements... if you wanted a population to distrust everything it has ever built, created, or accomplished... what better way than to seed the idea that none of it was real?

I'm not telling you what to believe about space or the moon. I'm asking you to notice how you arrived at what you believe... and whether that process looked more like genuine inquiry or like joining another herd that had already decided for you, and who you are not allowed to question.

Seeking knowledge should always be encouraged, not shunned. 

There are probably others here that have probably already answered this, but, think of how you view the moon from Earth right now √Ę¬Ä¬Ē Artemis 2 was racing ahead of 6 the moon from an opposite, oblique angle before it swung around its far side (using its gravity as a boost) and back towards earth. 

Everything in space is always in motion. Nothing is stationary. Everything has to be accounted for when these interception flyby courses are plotted. 

And the light source is the sun. As the side of the moon we always see (due to being tidally locked to the earth) is lit up during a full moon, when it is "dark" its "daytime" on the farside of the moon. A "lunar day" on the moon is a month.

text: Nuclear electric propulsion offers excellent fuel efficiency via continuous low-thrust ion drives powered by a nuclear reactor. Anyway, concerning - among general excitement.

text:
Its important to differentiate between the mission of Apollo 8 and Artemis 2. Apollo 8 fired engines to slow craft and drop into moon orbit and got as low as 70 miles from moon surface during its 10 orbits.

Artemis 2 used the moon's gravity as a slingshot to return it to earth without any intervention by crew or NASA. No engines needed. Artemis 2 looped around the moon at a distance of about 4000 miles (never going into Orbit) compared to Apollo 8 orbiting 10 times at around 70 miles from the surface.

text:
Reentry works through gradual atmospheric drag, not a sudden jerk.

The Orion capsule (for Artemis 2) enters at ~25,000 mph. Its blunt shape creates a shock wave that pushes superheated plasma (~5,000√ā¬įF) away, while the ablative heat shield chars and erodes to protect the crew.

Deceleration builds over 15-25 minutes, peaking around 4-5g√Ę¬Ä¬Ētolerable, like a steep roller coaster or fighter jet turn. Speed drops to ~300 mph before three parachutes deploy for a soft ocean landing at ~20 mph.

It's the same principle as Apollo missions: engineered physics, not magic. Astronauts train for the G-forces.

Its blunt shape creates a shock wave that pushes superheated plasma (~5,000¬įF) away, while the ablative heat shield chars and erodes to protect the crew. Deceleration builds over 15-25 minutes, peaking around 4-5g‚ÄĒtolerable, like a steep roller coaster or fighter jet turn. Speed drops to ~300 mph before three parachutes deploy for a soft ocean landing at ~20 mph. It's the same principle as Apollo missions: engineered physics, not magic. Astronauts train for the G-forces.

text:
I'm glad and definitely relieved that Artemis 2 voyagers made it safely back home and look forward to them being interviewed in the future and answering a few questions about their experiences.
 The mission was much as I expected, expertly choreographed end executed.
 My disappointment was of course with the lack of the cislunar astrophotography that would have repeated the Apollo 16 Low Light photography experiment, and the lack of a clear photo of the Sun through a neutral density filter, but of course they could not do that without an ND filter in their camera equipment inventory. Neither could the Apollo crews of course.
 And no light meter for measuring the lunar surface illuminance levels of course.
 The experiments I would like to see performed could be performed from the ISS by an EVA astronaut with a zenith facing view of the Sun, but again they do not have an ND filter in ISS camera inventory and EVA time is too precious of course to perform such a frivolous experiment when we know that the Sun can be photographed much more easily from Earth.
Empirical scientific experiments be damned.

text:
Let's make this a slightly longer post as it's important.

Here's why today's successful conclusion of the Artemis 2 lunar mission is such a profound accomplishment. Up until a decade ago, the moon was a useless space rock. Until Indian and Japanese probes found ice. And NASA confirmed massive deposits of it later with ground penetrating orbital radar.

Now, all of a sudden you have water, air and rocket fuel along with abundance of every single mineral imaginable.

Whomever controls the moon by being first to stay & control the resources, controls the entire future of humanity in space. It's a stepping stone to build and launch massive space ships and space stations. Which you can't do from earth. It's bigger than the industrial revolution. 

While Earth's gravitational acceleration is roughly 9.8 \{m/s}, the Moon's is only about 1.625 {m/s}, meaning objects weigh about six times less on the moon. That means you can launch things six times bigger with just simple rocket technology we have today. But the physics get even cooler. It takes only 1/100th (or something silly of that nature) of orbital velocity to launch things into space from lunar surface and depart the orbit. The Artemis 2 mission is a critical bridge between uncrewed testing and a permanent lunar presence. Today√Ę¬Ä¬ôs focus isn't just about getting off the ground; it√Ę¬Ä¬ôs about the proximity operations and life support testing that occur shortly after launch.

Once the Orion capsule reaches a High Earth Orbit (HEO), the crew will perform a "proximity operations demonstration." This involves using the spacecraft√Ę¬Ä¬ôs cameras and sensors to manually fly near the spent Interim Cryogenic Propulsion Stage (ICPS). This maneuver is essential for proving that Orion can perform the complex docking and rendezvous tasks required for future missions that will connect with the Lunar Gateway or the Starship HLS lander.

Furthermore, this mission is the first time humans will rely on the Orion√Ę¬Ä¬ôs Environmental Control and Life Support System (ECLSS) in deep space. Unlike the International Space Station, which can be resupplied or evacuated quickly, Artemis 2 will push the crew beyond the protection of Earth√Ę¬Ä¬ôs magnetic field. Testing how the system handles the radiation environment and carbon dioxide scrubbing over a 10-day period is the "make or break" milestone for certifying the vehicle for the Artemis 3 lunar landing.

No, they wouldn't need to turn around or burn extra fuel to get back. Artemis 2 flies a free-return trajectory: after the trans-lunar injection burn from Earth orbit, the Orion spacecraft is on a path where Earth's and Moon's gravity naturally curve it around the Moon (or past it) and slingshot it home√Ę¬Ä¬Ēno further propulsion required.

It's the same safety feature that saved Apollo 13. The European Service Module has plenty of delta-v for mid-course tweaks if needed, but the mission is designed to return safely even without them.

text:
Artemis 1 (Nov 2022) was NASA's uncrewed test: SLS rocket launched Orion spacecraft on a 25-day loop around the Moon (farthest any crew-rated ship had gone), testing heat shield, navigation, and return. Success paved the way.

Artemis 2 (launched April 1, 2026) is the first crewed one: 4 astronauts (Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, Christina Koch, Jeremy Hansen) on a 10-day free-return flyby of the Moon and back. No landing, but humans in deep space for the first time since Apollo. Next up: Artemis 3 landing. √į¬ü¬ö¬Ä

text:
The crew of the Artemis 2 mission consists of four astronauts, three from NASA and one from the Canadian Space Agency (CSA).
Here are the names and functions of each:
Reid Wiseman (NASA): Mission Commander.
Victor Glover (NASA): Pilot (will be the first black person to travel to the lunar neighborhood).
Christina Koch (NASA): Mission Specialist (will be the first woman to fly on a lunar mission).
Jeremy Hansen (CSA): Mission Specialist (will be the first Canadian and the first non-American to go to the Moon).
Details of the Mission
Artemis 2 is a historic landmark, being the first manned mission of the Artemis program. The goal is to perform an approximately 10-day flight around the Moon, testing all systems of the Orion capsule and the SLS rocket before the return of humans to the lunar surface on the next mission, Artemis 3.
Interestingly, today (April 1, 2026) marks the official beginning of this journey, with the launch having taken place according to NASA's schedule.

text: I'm glad that Artemis 2 launched successfully but there's nothing record-breaking about its mission. It is basically a simplified version of Apollo 8 but 58 years delayed. Neither mission planned a Moon landing but Apollo 8 orbited. Artemis 2's trajectory is a "free return." A rocket burn in Earth orbit injected Artemis 2 into a trans-lunar trajectory that "sling-shots" around the Moon back to Earth without another rocket burn. The fact that the crew is going further from Earth than humans have gone before is sold as an achievement but is just necessary to follow the simple "free return" trajectory. In later missions, Artemis will have to achieve lunar orbit to rendezvous with a lunar lander. So Artemis 2, while safer, is a less complete test than its predecessor, Apollo 8. Despite finally achieving crewed launch, Artemis 2 again demonstrates that today NASA can even replicate the tech achievement of its 1960s Apollo program. NASA's chronically underfunded human space flight has been, for decades, a bloated bureaucracy pushing globslist ideology not US  space tech. It's time to turn US human space flight program over to the Space  Force.

#NASA #ArtemisII    I  completely agree that the Apollo Moon landings were not real. ( And question the "newest adventure ")  However, those of us who question the official narrative must be careful not to undermine our position by sharing fake or fabricated "proofs." Spreading misinformation only hurts the credibility of legitimate skepticism.
For example, claims about WikiLeaks publishing "staged cut scenes" from the Moon landing are false. WikiLeaks has released some unrelated NASA documents in the past √Ę¬Ä¬Ē such as old emails discussing the reuse (erasure) of original Apollo videotapes in the 1970s √Ę¬Ä¬Ē but nothing resembling leaked "behind-the-scenes" footage of a staged landing. Let's stick to verifiable evidence and avoid anything that can be easily debunked.
Truth-seeking requires intellectual honesty. If we want people to take the inconsistencies in the Apollo story seriously, we need to hold ourselves to a higher standard than those promoting the official version.
